title: Lemon posset
subtitle: Set cream and lemon dessert
excerpt: Double cream heated with sugar and lemon juice until the acid sets the cream into a smooth, silky, intensely lemony dessert with no gelatine and almost no effort. Three ingredients and eight minutes of cooking. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Posset is a British drink of hot milk or cream curdled with wine or ale, documented from the medieval period. The modern lemon posset is a reinvention of the concept, using lemon juice as the curdling agent and serving it chilled as a dessert. The set is achieved through the reaction of the lemon acid with the cream proteins rather than with gelatine. Popularised in British restaurants and homes in the 2000s and documented by Simon Hopkinson and Lindsey Bareham in The Prawn Cocktail Years (1997) and by Delia Smith.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to two days ahead and refrigerate.

content.text: Lemon posset is chemistry made delicious. The lemon juice lowers the pH of the hot cream enough to partially denature the milk proteins, which form a network that holds the cream solid as it cools. No gelatine, no eggs, no water bath. The result is a cream that is both set and smooth, with an intensity of lemon flavour that is hard to achieve any other way.

Pour {{double-cream}} of double cream into a saucepan. Add {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar. Heat over medium heat, stirring until the sugar dissolves. Bring to a boil and boil for 3 minutes, stirring to prevent the cream from catching. Remove from the heat. Stir in the juice of {{lemon}} lemons. Leave to cool for 10 minutes. Pour through a fine sieve into a jug to remove any lemon seeds or stringy pulp. Pour into four small glasses or ramekins. Leave to cool to room temperature, then refrigerate for at least 4 hours until set. Serve with shortbread biscuits.

items.symptom: Posset not setting, still liquid after chilling
items.cause: Not enough lemon juice, or cream not boiled long enough.
items.fix: The cream must reach a rolling boil and stay there for 3 minutes. The lemon juice must be freshly squeezed — bottled lemon juice is less acidic and may not set the cream. Add the juice of half a lemon more and return the mixture to a brief boil.

items.cause: Cream overboiled, or lemon added while the cream was still at the boil.
items.fix: Remove the pan from the heat completely before adding the lemon juice. Adding acid to actively boiling cream can cause it to split rather than set smoothly.

items.cause: Cream percentage too high, or proportions wrong.
items.fix: Double cream at 48% fat sets firmly with lemon. For a lighter set, replace 100 ml of double cream with whipping cream.

content.text: Lemon posset works because double cream has a very high fat content (around 48%) and enough casein proteins to form a gel when acidified. The boiling step concentrates the cream and reduces its water content, which helps the set. The lemon juice drops the pH to around 4.6, the isoelectric point of casein, at which point the proteins aggregate and hold the cream solid. The higher the fat content of the cream, the more reliable the set.
